questions.question: What is the recommended way to handle Retrofit API errors in the repository layer?
options: Let raw exceptions bubble to the UI
Wrap calls in a safeApiCall helper that returns a sealed Result class
questions.explanation: Use a safeApiCall helper that wraps API calls and returns a sealed class (Success/Error/Loading). This prevents raw exceptions from reaching the UI layer.

questions.question: What is the difference between an OkHttp Interceptor and an Authenticator?
options: Interceptors see every request; Authenticators trigger specifically on 401 responses
Authenticators see every request; Interceptors trigger on 401
questions.explanation: Interceptors see every request/response and can modify them. Authenticators specifically trigger on 401 (Unauthorized) responses to refresh credentials and retry the request.

questions.question: When implementing retry logic with OkHttp, should you retry on 401 Unauthorized?
options: No, break on auth failures to avoid infinite loops
questions.explanation: Never retry on 401 Unauthorized in a retry interceptor — it can cause infinite retry loops. Use an Authenticator instead to handle token refresh specifically.

questions.question: What does OkHttp's addInterceptor() do vs addNetworkInterceptor()?
options: addInterceptor runs once per call; addNetworkInterceptor runs per network request including redirects
questions.explanation: Application interceptors (addInterceptor) run once per logical call. Network interceptors (addNetworkInterceptor) run for every actual network request, including redirects and retries.

questions.question: Which Kotlin approach is used for type-safe error handling with Retrofit results?
questions.explanation: Sealed classes like NetworkResult<T> with Success, Error, and Loading subtypes provide type-safe, exhaustive error handling that the compiler can verify.

questions.question: What library is commonly used with Retrofit for JSON serialization in Kotlin?
questions.explanation: Kotlinx Serialization (recommended for Kotlin-first projects) or Gson are the most commonly used JSON converters with Retrofit in Android development.

questions.question: How should you handle SocketTimeoutException in a network layer?
options: Ignore it since it rarely happens
Catch it as part of IOException handling and show a user-friendly timeout message
questions.explanation: SocketTimeoutException is a subclass of IOException. Handle it in your safeApiCall to show a user-friendly 'connection timeout' message instead of a generic error.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a base URL in Retrofit?
options: It defines the error handling strategy
It specifies the root URL for all API endpoints
questions.explanation: The base URL is the root URL of your API server. All endpoint paths defined in your Retrofit interface are resolved relative to this base URL.

questions.question: What is the correct way to inspect the error body from a failed Retrofit response?
questions.explanation: For non-successful responses, use response.errorBody()?.string() to read the error details. response.body() will be null for error responses.

questions.question: Why should error body parsing happen in the Repository layer, not the UI layer?
options: The UI layer doesn't have access to network classes
To maintain separation of concerns and keep the UI layer clean
It doesn't matter where you parse it
questions.explanation: Parsing error bodies in the Repository layer maintains separation of concerns. The UI layer should only receive clean, structured result objects (Success/Error) without dealing with raw HTTP responses.
